name: Horatius Cocles
description: Horatius Cocles was a legendary Roman hero who defended the Pons Sublicius, the only bridge across the Tiber River leading into Rome, against the invading Etruscan army led by Lars Porsena in the late 6th century BCE. According to tradition, Cocles, along with Spurius Lartius and Titus Herminius, initially held the bridge against a much larger force, allowing the Romans time to destroy the bridge behind them. When only Cocles remained, he stood alone, facing the Etruscans, and challenged them to single combat. He held them off long enough for the bridge to be destroyed. Then, weighed down by his armor, he leaped into the Tiber and swam to safety, though some accounts say he perished in the river. Horatius Cocles became a symbol of Roman courage, patriotism, and self-sacrifice. His bravery ensured the safety of Rome and prevented the Etruscans from capturing the city, solidifying his place as a pivotal figure in early Roman history and lore. His story is a cornerstone of Roman virtue and a testament to the willingness of Roman citizens to defend their homeland at any cost. His legendary stand has been retold and celebrated throughout history, inspiring generations with its message of unwavering dedication and valor.
subtitle: Defender of the Sublician Bridge
achievements: Single-handedly holding off the Etruscan army
Allowing the Romans to destroy the bridge
Swimming across the Tiber River in full armor
Becoming a symbol of Roman courage

keyMyths.title: The Defense of the Bridge
keyMyths.description: Horatius Cocles, along with two companions, bravely defended the Pons Sublicius against the advancing Etruscan army, buying time for the Romans to destroy the bridge and prevent the enemy from entering Rome. He stood firm against overwhelming odds, showcasing immense courage and tactical prowess.

keyMyths.title: The Swim to Safety
keyMyths.description: After the bridge was destroyed, Horatius Cocles, weighed down by his armor, leaped into the Tiber River and swam to safety, demonstrating incredible strength and determination. While some legends say he perished, the most popular version claims he reached the Roman side, a true hero.

keyMyths.title: Reward and Recognition
keyMyths.description: Upon his return to Rome, Horatius Cocles was celebrated as a national hero and rewarded with land and public acclaim for his extraordinary bravery. His actions solidified his place in Roman history as a symbol of courage, patriotism, and self-sacrifice.
